ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef bowl / gyudon entry; the specific variant with half boiled egg, diced and grated daikon, and ponzu sauce is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers beef and rice dishes broadly. The specific gyudon variant with ponzu, daikon, and half boiled egg is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for gyudon or this dish variant. No code assigned.

allergens: fish (ponzu contains citrus and may contain fish-based dashi)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with simmered sliced beef and onion, grated and diced daikon radish, a half boiled egg, and a drizzle of ponzu sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice, simmered beef (short plate / chuck), soft-boiled egg, daikon radish, and ponzu sauce; cross-referenced with Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition (MEXT 2020) for daikon and ponzu components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Japanese Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish variant. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 45% steamed white rice (USDA SR Legacy #20045), 25% simmered sliced beef with onion and soy-mirin sauce (USDA SR Legacy #13365 beef short plate, adjusted for cooking loss and sauce absorption), 10% half boiled egg (USDA SR Legacy #01129), 12% grated and diced daikon (MEXT 2020 item 06134), and 8% ponzu sauce (MEXT 2020 item 17028). Proportions are based on typical Sukiya gyudon serving composition. Values are rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (steamed, Japanese-style) GI ~64–72; mixed beef-rice bowl estimated at GI ~60–65 based on protein and fat co-ingestion attenuating rice GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for gyudon with ponzu, daikon, and half boiled egg. GI estimated at 62 based on: (1) steamed Japanese white rice GI ~68 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021); (2) protein (beef, egg) and fat co-ingestion reducing postprandial glucose response by approximately 8–10% per established mixed-meal attenuation literature (Wolever & Bolognesi 1996, Am J Clin Nutr); (3) daikon and ponzu contributing negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 87.75) / 100 ≈ 54.4, rounded to 54.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, mirin, and ponzu sauce
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (≈1,395 mg per 450 g serving), this dish provides a substantial portion of the 1,500–2,000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; those with hypertension should consume this dish infrequently and request reduced-sauce options where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index driven by white rice base, partially offset by protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 62 and glycemic load of approximately 54 per serving indicate a moderate postprandial glucose impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion size, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response, as the white rice base remains the primary glycemic driver.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and egg yolk combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 7.2 g saturated fat and ~1,395 mg sodium, both of which are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency and consider leaner protein substitutions or reduced-sauce preparations to lower saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Boiled Egg, Diced and Grated Daikon Gyudon with Ponzu Sauce is a Japanese beef rice bowl (gyudon) variant that layers thinly sliced beef simmered in a sweet-savoury soy-mirin-dashi sauce over a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The dish is distinguished by two forms of daikon radish — finely grated (oroshi) and diced — which add a crisp, mildly peppery freshness and digestive enzyme activity. A half boiled egg (hanjuku tamago) with a soft, jammy yolk is placed on top, contributing richness and additional protein. The entire bowl is finished with a drizzle of ponzu sauce, a citrus-soy condiment that introduces a bright, tangy counterpoint to the umami-rich beef. This variant is associated with the Japanese fast-food chain Sukiya and is available in Singapore via field survey. Nutritionally, a 450 g serving provides approximately 666 kcal, 35 g protein, 19 g fat, and 88 g carbohydrate, with notable sodium content (~1,395 mg) from the combined sauces. The dish is moderate on the glycemic index (estimated GI 62) due to the white rice base, though co-ingestion of protein and fat moderates the postprandial glucose response. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy, egg, wheat, or fish allergens. Clinically, the high sodium load is the primary concern for hypertensive individuals, while the moderate GI and saturated fat content are relevant for those managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in gyudon?
What is the GI of gyudon?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish variant. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA SR Legacy and MEXT 2020 Japanese food composition data. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI with mixed-meal attenuation. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the dish composition is well-characterised from the restaurant description.
notes: 1. food_group corrected from input value 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Mixed', as gyudon is a complete mixed rice bowl dish, not a condiment. 2. Nutrient values are component-estimated; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB/USDA entry for this exact dish would upgrade confidence to high. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component assembly and are not individually reported in a single approved source entry for this dish. 4. Halal status is unconfirmed; Sukiya Singapore has not been verified as halal-certified at time of processing. 5. The cuisine_tags in the input ('Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers', 'Drinks & Beverages', 'Fried Chicken') appear to be restaurant-level tags from field survey and are not specific to this dish; they have not been carried forward. 6. GI set to 62 as an analogous estimate; gi_category is medium. GL per serving = (62 × 87.75) / 100 ≈ 54.2.
